sources.short: Emerson Davis, Biographical Sketches of Congregational Pastors

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1758 minister at Walpole and Charlemont
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale in 1758 and was ordained at Walpole, New Hampshire on May 27, 1761. After 1767 he served the Charlemont, Massachusetts pulpit until 1785.

narrative_blocks.header: Uncle of Chief Justice Oliver Ellsworth
narrative_blocks.body: His sister Jemima Leavitt married Capt. David Ellsworth, making him the uncle of Oliver Ellsworth — Founding Father, signer of the Constitution, and third Chief Justice of the United States Supreme Court. The Ellsworth and Leavitt households were closely tied through Suffield, Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Dragged a runaway enslaved woman home by rope
narrative_blocks.body: In 1764 he was dismissed from his Walpole ministry after allegedly dragging a runaway enslaved woman home behind his horse by a rope tied to his saddle. He was installed at Charlemont, Massachusetts three years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Dismissed from Charlemont for his Loyalist sentiments
narrative_blocks.body: He was dismissed from his Charlemont, Massachusetts ministry in 1785 for Loyalist sympathies during the Revolution. He preached on alone in a schoolhouse and sued his former parishioners for back wages, winning roughly £700.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife died from medicine her daughter gave her
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Sarah Hooker Leavitt died in October 1791 at age 49 'as a consequence of taking wrong medicine' given to her by their daughter Clarissa. The obituary called the loss sudden and unexpected.

narrative_blocks.header: Two grandsons defended him in a 1904 book
narrative_blocks.body: Two of his grandsons John Hooker Leavitt and William Hooker Leavitt published a 1904 volume titled 'A Sketch of the Life and Character of Rev. Jonathan Leavitt.' They tried to portray their controversial ancestor as a misunderstood and complicated man.

education.notes: B.A. — Yale 1758. Tier-2 spouse of Sarah Hooker (H00302). Two brothers also Yale graduates. Nephew-by-marriage to Chief Justice Oliver Ellsworth.

cross_connections.display_label: Her descendant designed USS Hartford's steam engine
Her grandson sheltered fugitive Basil Dorsey five years

bio.notes: Sarah No. 2 — replacement naming after first Sarah (H00301) who died April 1741

narrative_blocks.header: Married Rev. Jonathan Leavitt of Heath
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. Jonathan Leavitt and moved to Heath, Massachusetts. Leavitt was twice dismissed from churches — once for dragging a runaway enslaved woman home by rope, and once for Loyalist sympathies.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children — eleven sons and one daughter
narrative_blocks.body: She had twelve children with Jonathan Leavitt — eleven sons and one daughter named Clarissa. EH lists only three; other sources confirm the full count of twelve.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter gave her wrong medicine, killing her
narrative_blocks.body: She died suddenly in 1791 after her daughter Clarissa gave her the wrong medicine. Clarissa herself died unmarried seven years later — never recovering from the accident.

narrative_blocks.header: Underground Railroad station owners descended from her
narrative_blocks.body: Two of her grandsons — Roger Hooker Leavitt and Hart Leavitt — operated Underground Railroad stations in Charlemont, Massachusetts, sheltering the fugitive Basil Dorsey for five years. Roger's home is on the National Park Service Underground Railroad Network to Freedom.

narrative_blocks.header: A wrong medicine, given by her daughter
narrative_blocks.body: She died suddenly at Heath on October 11, 1791, after taking the wrong medicine — handed to her, the Yale record says, by her daughter Clarissa. The funeral text was “He hath done all things well”; the Connecticut Courant carried the notice.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept the Hooker Elm's famous hospitality alive
narrative_blocks.body: He inherited the Farmington estate and the famous Hooker Elm from his father John. EH described him as 'a thorough gentleman of the old school' who continued the famed hospitality of the elm gatherings.

narrative_blocks.header: Favorite son and keeper of family history
narrative_blocks.body: He accompanied his father John on official journeys around the Colony and brought his first wife to live in the family house. EH wrote that he received from his father full information about the family's English origins — and that his father was the best informed of all Thomas Hooker's grandsons on the subject.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sisters married Hart brothers — knot tied
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah each married a son of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. Through those three marriages, Roger found himself simultaneously brother-in-law to Nathaniel, Samuel, and Matthew Hart — the Hart and Hooker families fully intertwined.

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughter Abigail married Samuel Talcott
narrative_blocks.body: His son Thomas Hooker fathered Abigail Hooker, who married Samuel Talcott of Hartford. The marriage merged the Hooker surname into the Talcott line — a transition that defines several gen 6+ branches.

parents.mother_research_notes: Sarah Bull — daughter of Thomas Bull, a Hartford Founder
parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Josiah Hart of Farmington — son of John Hart, grandson of Stephen Hart (X00034)
